problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem B (discrete optimization): river managers of the "Big Long River" (225-mile river, May 1–Oct 31 = 180-day season) must schedule rafting trips of 6–18 nights on either oar boats (4 mph) or motor boats (8 mph) so that at most one group occupies any campsite per night, with the goal of finding the maximum seasonal throughput ("carrying capacity") and a campsite-utilization/scheduling strategy. The team grounds the abstract river in the real Colorado River through the Grand Canyon to justify parameters (length, daylight, demand exceeding supply). Their headline answer: with 100 evenly-spaced campsites, ~1150–1250 boats per season (up to ~1400 if crowding is tolerated), reached by three independent models that converge on the same number.
models_used: Closed-form analytic base case: a deterministic 'conveyor belt' filling model. With identical boats, derives boats-per-day ΔX = floor[(E'-S')·v' / D'] (daylight window × speed ÷ campsite spacing, all rescaled to fractions of a day / fraction of river), plus exact send-off times s_i = E' - i·D'/v'. Includes a sensitivity analysis via partial derivatives ∂n/∂E, ∂n/∂S, ∂n/∂v, ∂n/∂D, noting the inverse-square sensitivity to campsite spacing D.

Model 1 — Agent-based / discrete-event simulation. 1-minute time steps over a full season; each boat is a selfish agent following local rules (first-come-first-served campsite claiming, no central planner). Daily distance goal g = (225 - d)/(r+1); boat starts hunting for a free site once it has been on water >8 h, or is within 2 h of sunset, or has passed its goal. Two failure criteria: travels past sunset, or >10 h on water. Inputs drawn from distributions: start time ~ Uniform(sunrise, 11am), speed ~ Bernoulli(0.5), trip length ~ discrete Uniform(6,18).

Model 2 — Itinerary model via a greedy heuristic scheduler. Central planner pre-assigns every boat's nightly campsite for the whole season. Targets g_i = i·L/r; sorts all boats by speed so 4 mph oar boats get scheduling precedence (least flexibility); each boat placed in the open campsite nearest its daily goal within range; boats that can't be placed (or can't finish in their duration) are dropped from the itinerary.

Model 3 — Probabilistic / stochastic model. Models each boat's desired daily stop as an exponential random variable ⇒ stop locations on the river as a Homogeneous Poisson Process; uses the order-statistic property that, conditioned on N arrivals over the river length, arrival positions are distributed as N i.i.d. Uniforms. Computes the probability a boat must travel an extra distance δ past its desired stop to find an open site as a sum of Binomial(N-1, k) 'blocking' events over the gaps w, w+D, w+2D… Extends to mixed fleets by inflating/deflating the danger window by p_f (probability a boat is a motor boat) to capture faster motor boats overtaking slower oar boats ('like prefers like' result).
key_techniques: Non-dimensionalization / rescaling: times as fractions of a 24 h day (S'=S/24), speed as fraction-of-river-per-day (v'=v·24/225), distances as fraction of river (D'=D/225) — makes the closed-form ΔX formula clean and lets probabilities map directly onto distances.

Real-world anchoring: using the Colorado River / Grand Canyon as a concrete analog to justify every assumption (225-mi length, civil-twilight daylight window at specific lat/long coordinates, demand > supply via the NPS weighted-lottery permit system).
Triangulation by three independent models that converge on ~1150–1250 boats/season — used explicitly as a robustness/validation argument ('multiple-model confirmation').
Defining 'carrying capacity' operationally as the largest ΔX keeping the failure rate under a 1% threshold, turning a fuzzy optimization into a measurable cutoff.
Sensitivity analysis via partial derivatives of the base model, identifying campsite spacing D (inverse-square) as the most sensitive parameter and reusing that insight to interpret the later models.
Poisson-process order-statistics trick: converting exponential daily-distance assumptions into a tractable uniform spatial distribution, then computing collision/blocking probabilities as binomials.

Optimization experiments inside the simulation: sweeping max start time and minimum nights for slow boats to drive down failure rate and push up utilization; 'overdriving' the greedy scheduler (ΔX>7) to trade a few dropped boats for higher total throughput (~1400).
Manager-facing framing: a one-page memo with three named, decision-conditioned proposals (Itinerary vs. Distance-Management) plus a costs/benefits section.
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective the paper's winning move is convergent triangulation: three genuinely different paradigms — a deterministic closed form, an agent-based simulation, and a rigorous stochastic/Poisson model — independently land on the same ~1150–1250 boats/season, which makes the headline number feel trustworthy rather than tuned. Each model is also conditioned on a different real-world assumption about boater behavior (do they follow an itinerary or not?), so the team answers the manager's actual decision problem instead of just reporting a number, and each model yields its own actionable insight (start early; give oar boats scheduling priority; keep fleet proportions constant). The probabilistic model in particular shows mathematical maturity rare at this level — exponential stops → Homogeneous Poisson Process → uniform order statistics → binomial blocking probabilities, with the mixed-fleet overtaking correction via p_f. Combined with disciplined non-dimensionalization, an explicit partial-derivative sensitivity analysis, a clean 1% operational definition of carrying capacity, and a polished manager memo, the paper demonstrates exactly the blend of rigor, robustness, validation, and communication that O-awards reward."
section_highlights: "Structure that judges reward: (1) A standalone one-page manager memo up front (To/Date/Subject) with three sections — Proposals, named decision-conditioned strategies; Costs and Benefits — written in plain business English with no math. (2) An explicit Parameters table and a layered Assumptions section split into Common Assumptions, a Base Case, and per-model assumptions, so the reader always knows what each model does and does not assume. (3) Approach built in increasing complexity: a simple closed-form base case first (with derivation and sensitivity analysis) that seeds intuition reused in every later model. (4) Each model gets a consistent Algorithm → Optimizations → Results sub-structure with figures (failure-rate vs. ΔX, utilization vs. ΔX, per-campsite utilization, total-boats curves). (5) A strong Conclusion that synthesizes all three models jointly, states the unifying 1150–1250 result, and lists Strengths/Weaknesses/Extensions honestly. The per-campsite utilization figure (Fig 8) showing low use of the first ~20 and last ~10 miles is a nice 'we examined our own output' touch."
pitfalls_or_limitations: All three models hinge on the strong assumption that campsites are perfectly evenly spaced; the team flags this as their biggest weakness but never actually relaxes it (only proposes it as future work).

Model 3's general blocking probability has no usable closed form — they only write out the one- and two-campsite (A/B) binomial cases and hand-wave the general term as 'cumbersome,' so the probabilistic estimate is effectively truncated.

The three models are not strictly comparable: they use different failure definitions (past-sunset / >10 h vs. unplaceable in itinerary vs. P(travel >14 mi) > 1%) and slightly different campsite counts (99 vs. 100), so the 'remarkable agreement' partly reflects loosely aligned metrics rather than identical ones.

Several figures in the markdown are clearly OCR/transcription-corrupted (e.g., Fig 7 'Max Total Trips Per Season' shows nonsensical year/total columns, Fig 19 probabilities exceed 1.0), making it hard to verify the plotted results — a reminder that the win rests on the narrative and derivations, not pixel-perfect data.

Model 3 assumes independence of each boat's daily desired distance and ignores correlated shocks like weather/storms (acknowledged) and assumes a strictly constant daily send-off mix, which is idealized.
The agent-based model's behavioral rules (8 h threshold, stop 2 h before sunset, goal formula) are plausible but somewhat arbitrary and not calibrated to real boater data.
Throughput is reported as a single point estimate band (1150–1250) without confidence intervals from the simulation, despite running multiple seasons.
'Demand always exceeds supply' removes any economic/queuing dimension — the model maximizes physical capacity, not value or fairness across customer types.
reusable_patterns: Triangulation template: attack one optimization quantity with 2–3 methodologically distinct models (deterministic closed form + simulation + probabilistic) and use their agreement as the validation argument — a reusable way to make a headline number credible.

Always build a closed-form base case first: it gives a sanity-check number, a non-dimensionalization scheme, and a partial-derivative sensitivity analysis whose conclusions you can reuse to interpret the harder models.
Non-dimensionalize early (fractions of a day, fraction of the route) to simplify formulas and let spatial probabilities equal distance fractions directly.
Anchor an abstract problem to a real-world analog (here Grand Canyon / Colorado River) to justify each assumption with a citation instead of asserting it.
Operationalize a vague target ('carrying capacity') as the largest control parameter keeping a failure metric under an explicit threshold (the 1% rule) so it becomes plottable and defensible.

Decision-conditioned recommendations: instead of one answer, give the client a small menu of strategies keyed to which assumption they believe (trust boaters' itinerary vs. not), each with costs/benefits.
Lead with a client-facing one-page memo in plain language before the technical body — separates 'what should I do' from 'how we know.'

Use the Poisson-process / uniform-order-statistics trick whenever you have exponential or memoryless spacing events along a line, then compute collisions as binomial blocking probabilities; extend to heterogeneous agents by widening/narrowing the danger window by a mixing probability (p_f for fast vs. slow).

Greedy heuristic with a priority ordering (schedule the least-flexible resource first — here the slower oar boats) is a simple, explainable scheduler that judges find convincing, and you can 'overdrive' it past capacity to explore a throughput-vs-rejection tradeoff.
Inspect your own simulation output for structure (per-campsite utilization revealed end-of-river avoidance) and report it as a credibility-building observation plus a caveat on aggregate metrics.
